C.R.A.F.T. PROMPT — OTOS × ASTRAZENECA ROUTE RESEARCH + BESPOKE AZ PDF BUILD BRIEF

C — CONTEXT

You are helping Dean Butler prepare the best possible AstraZeneca-specific route for OTOS.

A warm internal signal has emerged.

A contact at AstraZeneca — Emma, Head of Clinical Trials / clinical-trials route — has heard a short explanation of OTOS as being about:

ADHD and addiction, and joining up the pathway.

Emma has asked whether it is okay to put it under someone else’s nose at AstraZeneca.

This is significant because it may mean the material could be internally forwarded inside AstraZeneca.

Dean does not want to send a generic partner pack if a bespoke AZ-specific route would be stronger.

Dean already has an OTOS partner pack covering NHS, Public Health, CGL/CRS, Alcohol Liaison, RCE, Mind/WorkWell, Dr Claire/LAMP, Angel/Funder and Champion routes.

But AstraZeneca is different.

This is not an NHS partner brief.

This is not a local-authority cost-case brief.

This is not a clinical-provider pitch.

This is not asking AstraZeneca to endorse OTOS.

This is not asking AstraZeneca to enter ADHD prescribing, addiction treatment or clinical responsibility.

This is a strategic research and packaging task to identify the best AstraZeneca route and create a bespoke internal-forwarding PDF concept.

Potential AZ-relevant routes may include:

clinical-trials innovation;

patient engagement and retention;

decentralised / hybrid trial support;

digital health;

Evinova;

A.Catalyst Network;

external funding;

patient solutions;

health equity;

real-world evidence;

pathway optimisation;

integrated care pathways;

complex-cohort identification;

patient-reported outcomes;

longitudinal engagement;

under-served / hard-to-retain cohorts;

Cambridge innovation ecosystem;

public-private health system partnership.

OTOS core proposition:

OTOS Continuity is a non-clinical continuity layer.

It identifies where a person is at risk of being lost between services, keeps a light consent-led thread alive, surfaces drift and handoff failure, and gives human teams better visibility.

OTOS does not diagnose, prescribe, treat addiction, deliver therapy, replace clinical services, access NHS records, make clinical decisions or act as an EPR.

Core boundary:

OTOS surfaces signals. Partner teams make the decisions.

OTOS-specific thesis:

Adults with unrecognised or under-treated ADHD may self-medicate, develop addiction patterns, drift through services, relapse, disengage, return in crisis, and remain known to services but not held between services.

The same continuity problem may be relevant to broader complex patient cohorts, clinical-trial retention, patient engagement and pathway follow-through.

Dean’s lived pathway is relevant but must not dominate the AZ route.

The AZ brief should be internally forwardable, serious, strategic, non-emotional enough for corporate review, and precise enough that an AstraZeneca colleague can understand why it may be relevant without needing Dean in the room.

The task:

Research the AstraZeneca route properly.

Identify where OTOS could fit.

Decide whether a bespoke AZ PDF should be created now.

Define exactly what that PDF should say.

Build the full brief needed to produce the AZ PDF.

Prepare Emma / internal-forwarding language.

Avoid overclaiming, therapeutic-area mismatch or compliance risk.

Use current web research.

Use citations.

Prioritise official AstraZeneca, Evinova and credible life-sciences sources.

Do not invent AZ interest.

Do not imply AZ endorsement.

Do not claim ADHD/addiction is an AstraZeneca therapy-area priority unless verified.

Do not pitch OTOS as a drug, device, diagnostic or treatment.

Do not over-personalise the first AZ-facing asset.

Do not write the final designed PDF unless explicitly asked.

This task is research, route strategy, PDF architecture and internal-forwarding preparation.

R — ROLE

You are an elite UK life-sciences strategy consultant, pharma innovation adviser, clinical-trials transformation specialist, digital-health partnership strategist, patient-engagement expert, NHS / industry partnership analyst, health-equity adviser and investor-grade healthcare communications architect with 25+ years’ experience.

You understand:

AstraZeneca’s likely corporate routes;

pharma innovation and partnership funnels;

clinical-trial operations;

patient recruitment, retention and engagement;

decentralised and hybrid trials;

digital health and patient-reported outcomes;

real-world evidence;

pathway optimisation;

health-system partnerships;

commercial compliance sensitivities;

avoiding inappropriate disease-area claims;

how to create internal-forwardable corporate briefs;

how to package early-stage founder-led innovation for a large pharma audience.

You are not acting as a medical adviser.

You are not acting as AstraZeneca.

You are not claiming AZ has interest.

You are not writing promotional material.

You are building the safest, strongest route for a warm internal introduction.

# OTOS × AstraZeneca — Corrected Opportunity Strategy

This rebuild follows the corrected AZ brief: ADHD-fuelled addiction should not be demoted just because it is not an obvious AstraZeneca therapy-area fit; it should be tested as a white-space pathway, continuity, investment and public-private innovation opportunity. fileciteturn3file0

## 1. Corrected verdict

**Keep from the previous report:** AstraZeneca’s declared partnering areas are not primarily ADHD/addiction, so the route must not pretend AZ already has this category. AZ does, however, publicly signal interest in digital technologies, devices, diagnostics, patient solutions, patient-centric trials, external innovation, A.Catalyst, Evinova, external funding and health-system resilience. citeturn817508view0turn455412view0turn477597view2turn477597view5

**Reject:** The idea that therapy-area mismatch makes this weak. That is too flat. OTOS is not pitching an ADHD drug or addiction treatment. It is pitching a hidden, expensive, under-held cohort and a non-clinical continuity layer around it.

**Reverse:** Do not lead with “trial retention” alone. That makes OTOS sound generic. Lead with **ADHD-fuelled addiction as a hidden national cost chain**, then show why AZ routes may care: digital patient continuity, complex-cohort engagement, evidence generation, health equity, Cambridge innovation, external funding, public-private demonstrator and strategic optionality.

**New AZ thesis:** AstraZeneca may not “own” ADHD/addiction today. That is the strategic opening. OTOS gives AZ a way to look at an under-recognised cost chain without taking clinical responsibility for ADHD, addiction or prescribing.

## 2. New AZ master thesis

### Brutal internal line

**ADHD-fuelled addiction is already costing the system through A&E, detox, relapse, mental-health crisis, policing, prisons, unemployment and family breakdown. AZ may not own this category today — but that is exactly why it is white space.**

### AZ-safe corporate line

**OTOS Continuity is an early-stage, non-clinical pathway-continuity model designed to identify where adults with ADHD-linked addiction risk are being lost between services, keep a consent-led thread alive, and generate learning about whether better continuity can reduce costly late-stage failure.**

### Investor / driver line

**AstraZeneca has an opportunity to help test a new patient-continuity category: not a medicine, not a diagnostic, not addiction treatment, but a scalable pathway-intelligence layer around one of the NHS’s most expensive missed cohorts.**

## 4. ADHD-fuelled addiction white-space evidence

| Evidence area | What the evidence says | Why it matters to AZ | Why it matters to OTOS | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adult ADHD prevalence and hidden cohort | NHS England’s ADHD Taskforce cites ADHD prevalence at roughly 3–5%, while noting under-recognition and under-treatment. | This is not a fringe cohort. It is a common, system-wide condition with major downstream consequences. | OTOS can frame the cohort as already present across services but not held between them. | citeturn907950view0 |
| Unsupported ADHD creates cross-sector cost | NHS England links unsupported ADHD with educational failure, unemployment, crime, substance misuse, suicide, mental and physical illness, and at least £17bn in annual avoidable economic costs. | This gives AZ a national-cost, health-system resilience hook, not just a disease-area hook. | It validates the “hidden national cost chain” framing. | citeturn907950view0 |
| ADHD and substance misuse overlap | NHS England explicitly includes substance misuse among the adverse outcomes linked to unsupported ADHD. | This lets AZ see the ADHD/addiction route as a pathway failure, not a speculative personal story. | OTOS can position ADHD-fuelled addiction as a testable cohort hypothesis. | citeturn907950view0 |
| System failure shows up late and expensively | NHS England notes repeated A&E attendance, prisons, long-term sickness and unemployment as places where unsupported ADHD appears in the system. | This supports a public-private resilience route: earlier continuity may be cheaper than late crisis. | It gives OTOS the economic line: the system is already paying, just in the worst places. | citeturn907950view0 |
| ADHD waiting-list pressure | NHS England reports waiting times reaching more than four years for children and more than eight years for adults in some areas, with poor quality data and fragmented digital systems. | AZ can see a structural pathway gap where continuity, not prescribing, is the missing layer. | OTOS can sit around the wait, the handoff, the silence and the risk of relapse/disengagement. | citeturn907950view0 |

| ADHD medication demand and shortages | NHSBSA-referenced reporting shows ADHD prescriptions in England rising sharply, including lisdexamfetamine, with shortages linked to manufacturing issues and increased global demand. | The medication pathway is commercially and operationally significant even if AZ is not currently the ADHD manufacturer. | OTOS can frame the opportunity around pathway intelligence, adherence and support around constrained treatment access. | citeturn886142news1 |
| Lisdexamfetamine / Elvanse context | Public UK reporting identifies lisdexamfetamine as a major ADHD medicine in prescribing growth. US reporting identifies Vyvanse/lisdexamfetamine as Takeda’s ADHD drug and notes FDA approval of generic versions after exclusivity ended. This US position must not be transposed directly into UK market claims without MHRA/eMC verification. | This creates a strategic question: is there a support, persistence, sequencing or patient-continuity opportunity around ADHD medication pathways? | OTOS must not say “AZ should make Elvanse.” The safer opportunity is continuity around diagnosis, review, treatment access, adherence and relapse risk. | citeturn886142news1turn360727news0 |
| Medication persistence / continuation | Recent research coverage reports that ADHD medication use has grown substantially, while continuation after initiation remains low and varied. | A pharma/digital-health audience understands that initiation is not the same as persistence. | OTOS can explore “held through the pathway” rather than “given a prescription and lost again.” | citeturn886142news5 |
| Medication and broader outcomes | Observational research coverage reports ADHD medication use associated with reduced rates of substance misuse, suicidal behaviour, transport accidents and criminality. This should be treated as association, not OTOS proof. | It strengthens the strategic question: what happens if more people reach and stay within appropriate ADHD care? | OTOS can test whether continuity improves engagement with the right clinical doors. | citeturn256116news0 |
| Addiction / alcohol harm cost | Institute of Alcohol Studies reporting places alcohol harm costs in England at around £27bn annually, including health, crime, social care, unemployment and productivity losses. This is alcohol harm generally, not all ADHD-driven. | AZ sees the size of the downstream cost pool around addiction and relapse. | OTOS can responsibly say ADHD-fuelled addiction may be one contributing hidden chain within a much larger cost problem. | citeturn656142news0 |
| Criminal justice signal | Recent Cambridge-linked screening coverage reported high rates of possible undiagnosed ADHD among people arrested in London, including those arrested for drug offences. This is a screening signal, not diagnosis. | This supports the “known to systems but not held by pathways” argument. | OTOS can use it as a current watch-item, not a proof claim. | citeturn418949news0 |
| Trial-engagement relevance | Evinova states that nearly 80% of clinical trials fail to meet recruiting timelines and positions digital trial delivery around better patient experience, hybrid/decentralised models and trial efficiency. | A hard-to-retain, complex-cohort continuity model has relevance beyond ADHD/addiction. | OTOS can offer a human-continuity learning layer adjacent to trial engagement, not a validated trial product. | citeturn757242view1 |
| AZ route beyond therapy areas | AstraZeneca’s partnering material says interest extends beyond pharmaceuticals into diagnostics, devices, digital technologies and end-to-end patient solutions. | This is the door. ADHD/addiction may not be a core therapy area, but patient-continuity infrastructure can still be strategically relevant. | OTOS can frame itself as pathway intelligence, not a therapy-area claim. | citeturn407279view0 |

**Medication-market interpretation — AZ-safe version:**  
The lisdexamfetamine / Elvanse point should be included, but carefully. It is not a claim that AstraZeneca should manufacture Elvanse, acquire an ADHD medicine, or enter ADHD prescribing. The strategic point is this: ADHD treatment access, stimulant demand, shortages, initiation, persistence and follow-through are now major pathway issues. The stronger AZ opportunity is not “drug ownership”; it is **continuity, patient support, treatment sequencing, adherence intelligence, relapse-risk visibility and evidence generation around an under-held ADHD/addiction cohort**.

## 5. Commercial / strategic opportunity frame

| Opportunity layer | What it is | Why AZ should care | Proof needed | Risk control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Layer 1 — National-cost problem | ADHD-fuelled addiction as a hidden cost chain across addiction services, A&E, detox, rehab, mental-health crisis, housing, police, courts, prisons, welfare, employment and family breakdown. | It is a national health-system resilience problem, not a niche recovery-sector issue. AZ can explore a high-cost category before it is mature or crowded. | Evidence synthesis linking ADHD, substance misuse, service failure, relapse, crisis and cost. | Use “hypothesis”, “opportunity”, “hidden pathway”, not “proven causal savings”. |
| Layer 2 — OTOS demonstrator opportunity | A bounded Cambridge demonstrator to identify the cohort earlier, route to the right ADHD/addiction support, hold continuity, surface drift and produce a learning report. | It gives AZ a low-risk way to observe a new continuity category without taking clinical responsibility. | 12-week demonstrator design, consent flow, data dictionary, evaluation framework, partner map. | OTOS remains non-clinical; partner teams make decisions. |
| Layer 3 — AZ strategic upside | Category leadership, public-private innovation credibility, patient-continuity learning, Evinova adjacency, health-equity impact, Cambridge story, option-to-invest route. | AZ gets early visibility on a potentially investable pathway-intelligence category. | Demonstrator outputs showing engagement, drift reduction, route intelligence and partner usability. | No endorsement, no medical-device claim, no outcome guarantee, no therapy-area claim. |

## 6. AZ bait and hooks bank

| Hook | Best audience inside AZ | Why it works | Risk note |
|---|---|---|---|
| AZ may not own ADHD today. That is why this is white space. | Strategic Partnerships / BD | Turns mismatch into opportunity. | Do not imply AZ has an ADHD strategy. |
| This is not an ADHD app. It is a continuity layer around one of the NHS’s most expensive missed cohorts. | Clinical Operations / Digital Health | Separates OTOS from generic apps. | Support “expensive” with evidence, not assertion. |
| The system is already paying for ADHD-fuelled addiction — in A&E, detox, crisis care, courts, prisons and lost productivity. | Government Affairs / Health Equity | Makes the cost chain visible. | Phrase as “ADHD-fuelled may contribute to”, not total cost ownership. |
| OTOS does not ask AZ to treat addiction. It asks whether AZ wants to help prove the missing pathway layer. | External Funding / Sponsorships | Removes clinical-risk fear. | Keep “prove” as demonstrator/evidence generation. |
| Known to services is not the same as held between services. | Patient Experience | Simple, memorable, true to OTOS. | Do not overclaim outcome impact. |
| The failure is not always lack of services. It is loss between services. | NHS Partnerships | Positions OTOS as connective tissue. | Avoid blaming partners. |
| ADHD-fuelled addiction is a system problem hiding inside separate budgets. | Government Affairs | Speaks to cross-sector spend. | Needs public-health/economic support. |
| This is a pathway-intelligence opportunity, not a prescribing opportunity. | BD / Compliance | AZ-safe boundary. | Keep medication claims cautious. |
| The cohort is already visible after failure; OTOS is about seeing drift before failure. | Clinical Ops / Patient Engagement | Strong continuity logic. | “Before failure” should be framed as risk signal. |
| Waiting-list silence is not neutral for addiction-risk patients. | Health Equity / NHS Partnerships | Shows why continuity matters while waiting. | Do not claim OTOS manages clinical risk. |
| The opportunity is not to replace care, but to stop care falling through the gaps. | External Funding | Partner-safe. | Do not imply current services are negligent. |
| If ADHD is undertreated, addiction services may be working with a brain that cannot yet use the help properly. | Clinical / Health-System Leaders | Brutal truth, founder-aligned. | Phrase carefully in corporate materials. |
| A relapse can be a pathway failure, not only a personal failure. | Health Equity / Social Value | Reduces stigma; system lens. | Avoid sounding anti-recovery services. |
| OTOS turns silence into a signal. | Digital Health / Evinova | Very sharp product hook. | Clarify human review and safeguarding. |
| The first product is not a cure. It is visibility. | Evinova / Digital Health | Clear, modest, investable. | Do not call it validated. |
| A low-cost continuity layer may protect high-cost interventions from being wasted. | External Funding / BD | Commercial and health-economic logic. | Savings must be hypotheses. |
| ADHD/addiction is not a therapy-area pitch. It is a patient-continuity category. | Compliance / BD | Avoids therapy mismatch trap. | Keep this line central. |
| The white space sits between medication, recovery, mental health and public services. | Strategic Partnerships | Shows why no one owns it yet. | Avoid “AZ should own it” wording. |
| Cambridge can test this before the national system knows what to call it. | Cambridge DISC / A.Catalyst | Local innovation bait. | Keep pilot bounded and realistic. |
| AZ does not need to endorse OTOS to help test the category. | External Funding / Legal-sensitive readers | Lowers entry barrier. | Make governance explicit. |
| This is early, but the cost chain is not early. The system is already paying. | Investor / Sponsor Route | Strong urgency without overclaim. | Do not exaggerate OTOS maturity. |
| The demonstrator asks one serious question: can continuity reduce drift in a high-risk cohort? | Evidence / RWE | Testable, not fluffy. | Define metrics. |
| A.Catalyst exists for difficult ecosystem problems; this is one. | A.Catalyst / Innovation | Routes into AZ innovation language. | Cite A.Catalyst in formal asset. |
| Evinova is about patient-centric digital trial delivery; OTOS explores the human-continuity layer around hard-to-retain people. | Evinova | Creates adjacency without claiming fit. | Do not pitch as trial-ready. |
| The medication market tells us ADHD is no longer quiet; the pathway around it is still fragile. | Patient Support / BD | Medication-market hook without drug pitch. | Verify Elvanse/MAH before final PDF. |
| Treatment initiation is not the same as treatment continuity. | Patient Support / Digital Health | Pharma understands persistence. | Do not claim OTOS manages medicine. |
| A person can be diagnosed, referred, prescribed or discharged — and still be lost. | Patient Experience | Human, precise. | Avoid implying clinical teams are careless. |
| OTOS could give AZ an early option on the continuity layer, not the clinical liability. | Strategic Investment | Investor logic. | Use “could” and “option”. |
| This is not a charity ask. It is a category-discovery ask. | BD / Strategic Sponsors | Strong commercial framing. | Still include social-impact route. |
| The first win is not national rollout. It is a credible learning report that the right AZ team can interrogate. | Clinical Ops / RWE | Disciplined, credible. | Do not oversell pilot scale. |
| ADHD-fuelled addiction may be one of the most expensive problems hiding in plain sight. | Senior Sponsor | Strong top-line bait. | Use “may be” unless quantified. |
| If no one owns the gap, the organisation that helps define it first gains the strategic advantage. | Strategic Partnerships / Investor Route | Category-maker language. | Avoid “AZ should own this”. |
| OTOS surfaces signals. Partner teams make the decisions. | All audiences | Clean boundary. | Must be repeated in every asset. |

## 7. AZ-facing asset sequence

| Asset | Purpose | Length | Who sees it | When to use | Risk |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|
| Dean-to-Emma permission note | Give Emma safe language and permission to forward. | 120–180 words | Emma only | Send immediately. | Too long or too emotional weakens forwarding. |
| AZ one-page opportunity sheet | Sharp internal-forwarding bait: problem, white space, OTOS mechanism, AZ routes, ask. | 1 page | Emma and first AZ colleague | Emma forwards this first. | Must not look like a sales flyer or NHS procurement pitch. |
| Bespoke AZ PDF | Serious route brief for a second internal reader: why AZ, why white space, why demonstrator, what support route. | 6–8 pages | Clinical Ops / Evinova / A.Catalyst / External Funding / BD | Have ready immediately after the one-pager; send when Emma says someone is interested or when the first internal colleague asks for more. | Too much detail too early may slow forwarding. |
| Two-page evidence memo | Evidence spine: ADHD burden, substance misuse link, medication demand, addiction cost, trial engagement. | 2 pages | Evidence/RWE/clinical ops readers | Use after interest, before meeting. | Needs clean citations and no causal overclaim. |
| Demonstrator outline | Defines the 12-week Cambridge learning exercise, outputs, data, governance and budget. | 2–4 pages | Funding, A.Catalyst, Government Affairs, External Sponsorships | Use when someone asks “what exactly would AZ back?” | Must not imply live clinical pilot before governance. |
| Governance and boundary sheet | Shows OTOS is non-clinical, consent-led, safeguarding-aware, data-protection-aware. | 1–2 pages | Compliance-sensitive readers | Use before any formal AZ discussion. | Missing this makes OTOS look risky. |
| Route-specific email variants | Tailored approach for Clinical Ops, Evinova, A.Catalyst, External Funding, BD. | 150–250 words each | Dean / Gareth / Emma route | Use after target identified. | Generic wording wastes the warm route. |
| Full OTOS partner pack | Broad partner context. | Existing pack | Only after AZ asks for wider context. | Not first. | Too local/NHS-heavy and not AZ-specific. |
| Public Health cost case | Shows economic depth. | Existing / appendix | After AZ shows interest in health-system resilience or funding. | Not first. | Can feel like local authority pitch. |
| Angel / investor brief | Shows investment logic. | Existing / adapted | Only if BD/strategic investment asks for investment case. | Not first. | May feel founder-funding-led rather than AZ-opportunity-led. |
| Personal clinical letter | Deep founder proof/context. | Private | Only if explicitly relevant and carefully controlled. | Not first. | Too personal and clinically sensitive for internal forwarding. |
| Raw business plan | Full venture detail. | Long form | Later due diligence only. | Do not send first. | Too much, wrong format, risks idea leakage. |

**Exact sequence now:**  
Emma gets the permission note plus the one-page AZ opportunity sheet. Emma forwards the one-page sheet internally. Dean keeps the 6–8 page AZ PDF ready for the first serious reply. The evidence memo, governance sheet, demonstrator plan and investment case wait until the right AZ route is identified.

Partner teams make decisions.” |

## 9. AZ one-page opportunity sheet

**Headline**  
**The ADHD-Addiction Continuity Gap: a white-space patient-continuity opportunity for AstraZeneca**

**Three-line problem**  
Unsupported ADHD is linked with substance misuse, crisis demand, unemployment, criminal justice exposure and avoidable economic cost.  
Addiction services, ADHD services, mental health, public health and justice often see the same person at different stages — but no one holds the thread between them.  
The system is already paying for this failure, but often at the crisis end.

**Three-line opportunity**  
OTOS proposes a bounded, non-clinical continuity demonstrator around ADHD-fuelled addiction risk.  
It does not diagnose, prescribe, treat addiction, make clinical decisions or access NHS records.  
It keeps a consent-led thread alive, surfaces drift, supports handoff visibility and produces pathway-learning intelligence.

**Why AstraZeneca**  
AstraZeneca may not currently own ADHD/addiction as a therapy area — that is the white-space opportunity. AZ has relevant strategic routes in digital health, patient experience, clinical-trial engagement, external innovation, health equity, public-private partnership, Cambridge innovation and strategic partnering.

**Why now**  
ADHD service pressure, medication demand, long waits, addiction cost and digital-health transformation are converging. The question is not whether AZ has an ADHD product today; it is whether AZ wants early visibility on a hidden national pathway category.

**What OTOS does**  
Micro check-ins. Appointment reminders. Transition receipts. Consent-led touchpoint logging. Drift/silence visibility. Human review queue. Partner handoff visibility. Learning report. Route intelligence.

**What OTOS does not do**  
No diagnosis. No prescribing. No addiction treatment. No therapy. No clinical decisions. No replacement of NHS, recovery or clinical services. No AZ endorsement assumed.

**Possible AZ routes**  
Clinical Operations review for complex-cohort retention.  
Evinova/digital-health review for patient-continuity architecture.  
A.Catalyst/Cambridge innovation review for demonstrator incubation.  
External Funding/Sponsorships review for a bounded evidence demonstrator.  
Health Equity/Social Value review for underserved neurodivergent/addiction cohort impact.  
Business Development/Strategic Partnerships review for strategic investment or option-to-invest.

**The ask**  
Please route this to the AZ internal owner best placed to assess whether a 12-week Cambridge demonstrator, strategic sponsorship, external funding route or digital-health exploration is worth a serious exploratory conversation.

**Boundary line**  
OTOS surfaces signals. Partner teams make the decisions.

## 10. Emma / internal-forwarding messages

### Dean to Emma — stronger but safe

Hi Emma — yes, absolutely fine to put this under the right nose internally.

I have pulled the AZ angle into a short, AZ-specific opportunity sheet rather than sending a generic OTOS partner pack. The reason is that this is not a request for AstraZeneca to endorse OTOS, enter addiction treatment, prescribe, diagnose, or “own ADHD” in a therapy-area sense.

The stronger question is whether ADHD-fuelled addiction is an under-recognised national cost chain where a non-clinical continuity layer could be tested. OTOS is early-stage, but the pathway failure is real: people are known to services without being held between services.

The most relevant internal routes may be Clinical Operations, Evinova/digital health, A.Catalyst/Cambridge innovation, External Funding/Sponsorships, Health Equity/Social Value, Government Affairs/NHS Partnerships or Strategic Partnerships.

The ask is for route ownership and exploratory review — not endorsement.

### Emma to AZ colleague — internal-forwardable

I’m sharing this because it may sit outside AstraZeneca’s obvious therapy-area framing, but that may be the point.

OTOS is an early-stage, non-clinical continuity concept focused on ADHD-fuelled addiction as a hidden pathway/cost problem: people cycling through ADHD, addiction, mental-health, A&E and justice/public services without being held between services.

This is not a drug, diagnostic, treatment or endorsement ask. The possible AZ relevance is around patient continuity, complex-cohort engagement, digital health, clinical-operations learning, health equity, external innovation, Cambridge ecosystem and potentially a bounded evidence demonstrator.

The pointed question is: should this be reviewed by Clinical Operations, Evinova/digital health, A.Catalyst/Cambridge innovation, External Funding/Sponsorships, Health Equity/Social Value, Government Affairs/NHS Partnerships or Strategic Partnerships?

### Dean to introduced AZ contact

Thank you for taking a look.

I want to be clear from the start: OTOS is not asking AstraZeneca to endorse us, enter addiction treatment, diagnose ADHD, prescribe medication or take clinical responsibility. OTOS is a non-clinical continuity and pathway-intelligence layer.

The opportunity I would like to explore is whether ADHD-fuelled addiction represents a hidden, high-cost pathway category where a bounded demonstrator could test whether better continuity reduces drift, improves handoff visibility and creates useful learning for health-system partners.

The strongest AZ routes may be Clinical Operations, Evinova/digital health, A.Catalyst/Cambridge innovation, External Funding/Sponsorships, Health Equity/Social Value or Strategic Partnerships. I would value a focused conversation on which internal route could properly assess this as an early-stage but serious opportunity.

### Ultra-short “under someone’s nose” note

This is not an ADHD app or addiction-treatment pitch. It is a non-clinical continuity layer around a hidden cost chain: ADHD-fuelled addiction and the people lost between services. AZ may not own this category today — that may be the white-space opportunity.

### If AZ asks “Why us?”

Because AstraZeneca has credible routes that go beyond therapy-area ownership: patient-centric digital health, clinical-operations innovation, Evinova, A.Catalyst, external funding, health equity, Cambridge innovation, and strategic partnerships. OTOS is not asking AZ to own ADHD/addiction; it is asking whether AZ is the right organisation to help test a missing continuity layer around an expensive, under-recognised pathway failure.

### If AZ asks “We don’t do ADHD/addiction”

That is understood, and the brief does not claim otherwise. The opportunity is not “AstraZeneca should enter ADHD/addiction as a therapy area.” The opportunity is that ADHD-fuelled addiction may be a white-space continuity category sitting between services, medicines, recovery, mental health and public systems. That makes it potentially relevant to patient-continuity infrastructure, digital-health learning, public-private innovation and strategic sponsorship.

### If AZ asks “Is this a drug pitch?”

No. OTOS is not a medicine, diagnostic, prescribing pathway or treatment. The medication context matters only because ADHD treatment access, demand, shortages, initiation and persistence show that the pathway is under pressure. The opportunity is continuity, support, handoff visibility, adherence intelligence and learning — not a claim that AZ should manufacture or promote any ADHD drug.

### If AZ asks “Is this asking for funding?”

Not as the first ask. The first ask is internal route ownership: who inside AZ should assess this properly? A later route could be external funding, sponsorship, A.Catalyst incubation, Evinova exploration, co-designed evidence demonstrator, strategic partnership or option-to-invest — but only after the right AZ team agrees the category is worth exploring.

## 12. Investment / sponsorship thesis

| Support model | What AZ gives | What AZ gets | Risk | Best first ask |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small strategic grant | Limited funding for one-page evidence build, demonstrator design or feasibility work. | Low-risk early visibility on a white-space category. | Too small to build meaningful evidence if under-scoped. | “Could AZ’s external funding route support a bounded feasibility/evidence package?” |
| External funding / sponsorship | Funding for a defined 12-week Cambridge demonstrator and learning report. | Public-private health-system impact without therapy-area ownership. | Compliance and independence concerns. | “Can this be reviewed as a non-promotional healthcare ecosystem collaboration?” |
| A.Catalyst incubation | Innovation route, mentoring, partner access, ecosystem validation. | Early-stage category exploration aligned with external innovation. | May require more maturity. | “Can A.Catalyst assess this as a Cambridge pathway-continuity demonstrator?” |
| Evinova exploration | Digital-health architecture review, patient-experience challenge framing. | Learning on hard-to-retain, complex-cohort continuity adjacent to trial engagement. | OTOS is not trial-ready. | “Can Evinova review whether the continuity model has relevance to patient-engagement architecture?” |
| Strategic partnership | AZ helps define evidence questions, route, governance and scaling logic. | Early category influence and optionality. | Could appear like endorsement if poorly worded. | “Can AZ nominate a strategic owner to assess whether this category merits structured exploration?” |
| Corporate social value / health equity funding | Support for under-served neurodivergent/addiction cohort demonstrator. | Social impact, access, equity and system-resilience story. | Could be seen as philanthropy only. | “Can Health Equity/Social Value review this as an underserved-cohort continuity intervention?” |
| Public-private pilot funding | AZ contributes to a matched pilot with NHS/public health/VCSE/philanthropic partners. | Stronger legitimacy and shared risk. | Slower, more governance-heavy. | “Can AZ consider match-funding or co-sponsoring a public-private learning pilot?” |
| Co-designed evidence demonstrator | AZ helps define metrics, evidence standards and learning-report outputs. | Higher-quality evidence and clearer investment decision later. | Evidence design may expose gaps. | “Can RWE/outcomes colleagues advise on what proof would make this investable?” |
| Strategic investment / option to invest later | AZ gets early option, observer rights or structured follow-on route. | Upside if OTOS becomes a patient-continuity category. | Too early unless first evidence is strong. | “Can BD/Strategic Partnerships review this as an early option-to-invest category after demonstrator scoping?” |
| Match-funding with NHS/Public Health/philanthropy | Shared funding pool for lower-risk demonstrator. | Shared credibility; no single party owns risk. | Coordination complexity. | “Can AZ join as one of several backers around a bounded Cambridge evidence demonstrator?” |

## 13. Proof and evidence roadmap

| Evidence / asset | Status | Use in AZ pitch? | Risk | Next action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Corrected AZ white-space thesis | Ready conceptually. | Yes — central. | Could sound too bold without boundary. | Use as the one-page headline frame. |
| OTOS non-clinical mechanism | Ready in principle. | Yes. | Needs simple visual proof. | Build one clean mechanism diagram. |
| Founder lived pathway | Available. | Lightly, not first-page dominant. | Too personal if overused. | Use one restrained founder line only. |
| ADHD Taskforce evidence | Available from NHS England. | Yes. | Must not overextend beyond source. | Pull 3–5 cited facts into evidence memo. |
| ADHD/substance misuse link | Available at system level. | Yes. | Needs care around causality. | Use “linked with” and “cohort hypothesis”. |
| Alcohol/addiction cost context | Available from public sources. | Yes, as context. | Not all alcohol cost is ADHD-driven. | Label as wider downstream cost pool. |
| Medication demand / shortages | Available from public reporting. | Yes, carefully. | Risk of sounding like drug pitch. | Frame as pathway-pressure evidence. |
| Elvanse/lisdexamfetamine ownership / MAH | Partially public; needs verification. | Only as cautious context. | UK-specific ownership errors would damage credibility. | Verify with eMC/MHRA before final PDF. |
| Generic lisdexamfetamine / patent context | Public US context available. | Only as strategic question. | US facts must not be applied to UK without checking. | Keep in appendix or internal note. |
| Existing OTOS partner pack | Available. | Not first. | Too generic and local-partner heavy. | Hold for later context. |
| Public Health cost case | Available / developing. | Later. | Can feel like LA pitch. | Convert into AZ-safe economic appendix. |
| Clinical letter / personal medical evidence | Available privately. | Not first. | Confidentiality and over-personalisation. | Use only if Dean chooses and recipient is appropriate. |
| Partner map | Partly available. | Yes, high-level. | Must not imply commitments. | Create “engagement map” with status labels. |
| Cambridge demonstrator plan | Needs tightening. | Yes, after one-pager. | Without scope it sounds abstract. | Define 12-week pilot, cohort, outputs and budget. |
| Data-protection outline | Needs drafting. | Yes before serious meeting. | AZ will worry about data. | Produce one-page DPIA-lite summary. |
| Consent model | Needs drafting. | Yes. | Consent ambiguity kills credibility. | Build consent-led touchpoint flow. |

### Assistant output 12 — 2026-06-02T00:56:14.889447+00:00 — gpt-5-5-thinking

Yes — understood. That was **Claude reporting back**, not you authorising it as the new truth.

My feedback: **Claude has moved the route in the right direction, but I would not treat that v3 as authority yet.** It is a strong candidate draft, not the locked OTOS/AZ position.

The best parts are worth keeping: it correctly moves away from the weak “white-space continuity” frame and towards **NIA as a founder-originated proposed category**; it protects the term by saying “proposed, not an official diagnosis”; it preserves the DTD → NIA lineage; and it correctly understands that the AZ opportunity must be **category ownership / medicine-plus-continuity / evidence / investment**, not just “please route this internally”. That is the strategic upgrade. fileciteturn13file0

The medication-pathway correction is also mostly right: Vyvanse/lisdexamfetamine is Takeda’s ADHD drug, Reuters confirms generic versions were approved after Takeda lost exclusivity, and the US raised production limits for Takeda’s drug and generics because of shortage pressure. citeturn596177view0 UK shortage pressure is also real: public reporting quotes the Government’s 2023 national patient safety alert as saying disruption was caused by manufacturing issues and increased global demand, and that lisdexamfetamine was among the ADHD medicines affected. citeturn878025news0

But I see **five problems** before we accept it.

First, **“category ownership” is right, but “AZ could own the category” needs careful phrasing**. We can say AZ could help define, evidence, sponsor, invest in, explore or take early strategic position in NIA. We should avoid making it sound like AZ can “own” a lived condition or patient group. The stronger safe phrase is:

> **AZ could take early strategic position in the NIA category before the market has formally named, evidenced or commercialised it.**

Second, **BD as the first route may be too corporate too soon**. Claude ranks Strategic Partnerships / BD first. I partly agree, but for Emma’s warm clinical-trials route, the first internal forward may land better as:

> **A.Catalyst / Cambridge innovation + Evinova / digital health + BD as the strategic overlay.**

That way it does not look like Dean is walking in asking for investment before evidence. The real route is: **innovation door first, BD/investment door close behind**.

Third, **the “medicine-plus-continuity” line is excellent, but it needs one extra guardrail**. Claude says “never AZ should make Elvanse”, which is right. I would add:

> **NIA may create future therapeutic optionality, but the first AZ ask is not molecule ownership. It is category review, pathway evidence and continuity infrastructure.**

That keeps the landgrab alive without sounding reckless.

Fourth, **the phrase “cohort holds the name / owns the first proof cohort” is too strong legally and scientifically**. OTOS can own the terminology, story, framework, software and evidence layer. It cannot “own” the cohort. Better:

> **OTOS owns the founder-originated terminology, the Connected Pathway concept and the first demonstrator architecture for the ADHD-linked NIA cohort.**

Fifth, **some cited figures still need a verification lock before going into a live PDF**. ### Assistant output 15 — 2026-06-02T01:19:24.403580+00:00 — gpt-5-5-thinking

My feedback: **v4 is much closer. It is now strategically alive.** But I still would not lock it as final authority until we make four precision edits.

The big improvement is right: it moves from “continuity opportunity” to **NIA + lisdexamfetamine + first-mover category + medicine-plus-continuity + OTOS infrastructure**. That is the right shape. The document explicitly says: NIA is the category, lisdexamfetamine is the lived proof point, medicine-plus-continuity is the intervention model and OTOS is the infrastructure. fileciteturn14file0

## My verdict

**Strategic direction: 9/10.**  
**Language power: 8.5/10.**  
**Safety/precision: 7.5/10.**  
**Ready to build from: yes, after four edits.**  
**Authority status: strong candidate, not locked yet.**

## The four edits I would make before the one-pager

### 1. Replace “proof point” with “lived proof point / pathway proof point” almost everywhere

“Proof point” alone can sound like clinical proof. Use:

> **lived proof point**

or:

> **pathway proof point**

Best line:

> **Lisdexamfetamine is Dean’s lived proof point that ADHD medication stability can be the missing prerequisite that allows recovery to land.**

This keeps it loud without implying it proves efficacy for everyone.

### 2. Make “first-mover / new market share” stronger, but less blunt

“New market share” is strategically right, but in the first AZ-facing asset it should read more professionally:

> **first-mover advantage in a future medicine-plus-continuity market**

Then later:

> **new market-share potential**

Use “new market share” in the investor / BD language, not the first public-facing sentence.

### 3. Do not imply AZ can enter lisdexamfetamine too easily

The v4 says branded generic / licensing / acquisition routes are investigation hypotheses, which is correct. Keep that. Reuters supports the core facts: Vyvanse/lisdexamfetamine is Takeda’s, generics were approved after Takeda lost exclusivity, and the DEA increased production limits for Vyvanse and generics because of shortage pressure. citeturn831676news0

So the exact safe wording is:

> **AstraZeneca should not be asked to “make Elvanse”. The strategic question is whether AZ wants to explore the wider medicine-plus-continuity category around ADHD medication stability, including future patient-support, companion digital, formulation, licensing, branded generic or acquisition routes subject to regulatory, IP and commercial review.**

That is strong and safe.

### 4. Move Patient Support / Adherence higher

Claude ranks BD first, A.Catalyst second, Patient Support third. I would slightly reframe the route stack:

**First internal read:** BD / Strategic Partnerships  
**Most natural medicine door:** Patient Support / Adherence / Persistence  
**Safest demonstrator home:** A.Catalyst / Cambridge  
**Digital scale route:** Evinova  
**Evidence route:** RWE

So the ask becomes:

> **Please route this for a first-mover NIA category review, with parallel consideration by Patient Support / Adherence and A.Catalyst for a Cambridge evidence demonstrator.**

That stops it sounding like “just investment”.

## The best upgraded lead paragraph

Use this as the master opener:

> **NIA — Neurologically Informed Addiction — is a founder-originated proposed category before the market has named it. It describes addiction driven or amplified by underlying neurology, especially ADHD. In Dean Butler’s lived case, lisdexamfetamine was the stabilising medication that allowed recovery to finally land. OTOS is the Connected Pathway that holds the person around that intervention: diagnosis, access, adherence, persistence, relapse-risk visibility, behavioural reinforcement and service connection.**
>
> **This is not a single-molecule pitch. It is a first-mover opportunity in a future medicine-plus-continuity category.**

That should lead the one-pager.

## Best one-pager title

I would not use “A New Category Before the Market Names It” as the main title alone. It is strong but slightly start-up-sounding.

Better:

# **NIA: The Medicine-Plus-Continuity Category Beneath Relapse**
## **A first-mover opportunity in ADHD-linked addiction, medication stability and connected recovery infrastructure**

Alternative, sharper:

# **NIA: A First-Mover Category Opportunity**
## **Lisdexamfetamine, medication stability and the Connected Pathway behind ADHD-linked addiction**

Best for AZ:

# **NIA: A First-Mover Category Opportunity**
## **The medicine-plus-continuity pathway behind ADHD-linked addiction**

## Images: my feedback

The image brief in v4 is right to avoid faces, pills, molecule clichés and fake pharma imagery. The first three images should be:

1. **NIA category triangle**  
   New Category / New Intervention / New Infrastructure. This is the whole strategy in one picture.

2. **Lisdexamfetamine lived proof pathway**  
   Not a pill image. Use an abstract line: chaotic loop → medication stability → recovery can land → OTOS keeps the pathway held.

3. **OTOS Connected Pathway**

The key instruction for the image generator/designer should be:

# C.R.A.F.T. PROMPT — FINAL AZ ONE-PAGE BRIEF  
## OTOS × AstraZeneca / NIA First-Mover Category + Medicine-Plus-Continuity Opportunity

## C — CONTEXT

You are helping Dean Butler finalise the **AstraZeneca one-page opportunity brief** for OTOS.

This is now a high-importance, one-shot strategic asset.

A previous strategy document exists:

**AZ × OTOS / NIA — First-Mover Category + Medicine-Plus-Continuity Strategy (v4)**

Treat v4 as the **working base**, not as locked final authority.

v4 correctly upgraded the old weak frame.

The old weak frame was:

- white-space continuity;
- hidden ADHD-addiction cost chain;
- internal routing;
- “not a drug pitch”;
- “not a funding demand”;
- “put this under the right internal nose”.

That is no longer enough.

The corrected strategic frame is:

# **NIA — Neurologically Informed Addiction**
## **A founder-originated proposed category before the market has named it**

Dean Butler / OTOS has coined the proposed terminology:

> **NIA — Neurologically Informed Addiction**

NIA describes addiction driven or amplified by underlying neurology, especially ADHD-linked executive dysfunction, impulsivity, emotional dysregulation, dopamine-seeking self-medication, poor treatment retention, relapse and recurring public cost.

NIA is **not currently an official diagnosis**.

It must be framed as:

- founder-originated proposed terminology;
- coined by Dean Butler / OTOS;
- evolved from Dean’s earlier term **Double Trouble Diagnosis / DTD**;
- a proposed category;
- a proposed condition class;
- a service-design and commissioning lens;
- a research category;
- a commercial category;
- a medicine-plus-continuity opportunity;
- a future recognition opportunity.

It must **not** be presented as a currently recognised ICD/NICE/NHS diagnosis.

Core founder line:

> **I did not invent the suffering. I named the pattern.**

Core strategic line:

> **NIA is a new category before the market has named it. Lisdexamfetamine is the lived proof point. OTOS is the infrastructure that can evidence, hold and scale the medicine-plus-continuity pathway around it.**

But before building the one-pager, you must apply these four corrections to v4:

## Required correction 1 — “Proof point” language

Do not use “proof point” in a way that implies clinical proof.

Use:

- **lived proof point**
- **pathway proof point**
- **founder lived proof point**

Preferred line:

> **Lisdexamfetamine is Dean’s lived proof point that ADHD medication stability can be the missing prerequisite that allows recovery to land.**

Do not imply lisdexamfetamine proves efficacy for everyone.

## Required correction 2 — First-mover / market-share language

Do include first-mover advantage and new market-share potential.

But make the first AZ-facing version professional.

Preferred phrase:

> **first-mover advantage in a future medicine-plus-continuity market**

Use “new market-share potential” mainly for BD / investor / strategic commercial language.

Do not make the opening sound like a shallow start-up market grab.

Make it serious, category-led and pharma-aware.

## Required correction 3 — Lisdexamfetamine route discipline

Lisdexamfetamine must be loud.

It is central.

It is the reason Dean is “out the other side”.

But do **not** say AstraZeneca can simply “make Elvanse”, “brand lisdexamfetamine” or enter the market without review.

Correct framing:

> **AstraZeneca should not be asked to “make Elvanse”. The strategic question is whether AZ wants to explore the wider medicine-plus-continuity category around ADHD medication stability, including future patient-support, companion digital, formulation, licensing, branded generic or acquisition routes subject to regulatory, IP, MHRA and commercial review.**

Must mention:

- lisdexamfetamine;
- Elvanse / Vyvanse context;
- Takeda ownership where verified;
- genericising context where verified;
- ADHD medication shortage / demand context where verified.

But all branded generic / licensing / acquisition / formulation possibilities must be labelled as:

- strategic question;
- investigation hypothesis;
- commercial optionality;
- subject to regulatory, IP, MHRA, supply and commercial review.

## Required correction 4 — Route stack refinement

Do not make the ask look like “just investment”.

Re-rank the practical AZ route stack as:

1. **Strategic Partnerships / Business Development** — category and first-mover review.
2. **Patient Support / Adherence / Persistence** — medication stability and pathway support.
3. **A.Catalyst / Cambridge Innovation** — demonstrator incubation.
4. **Evinova / Digital Health** — companion digital / Connected Pathway architecture.
5. **Real-World Evidence / Outcomes** — evidence engine.
6. **External Funding / Sponsorships** — bounded demonstrator support.
7. **Health Equity / Social Value** — underserved high-cost cohort impact.
8. **Government Affairs / NHS Partnerships** — public-private pathway pilot.

This keeps the landgrab strong while avoiding a crude funding/investment ask.

## Core strategic proposition

The AZ opportunity is now a **three-pronged offer**:

# **New Category · New Intervention Model · New Infrastructure**

## 1. New Category

**NIA — Neurologically Informed Addiction**

A founder-originated proposed category for addiction driven or amplified by underlying neurology, especially ADHD.

## 2. New Intervention Model

**Medicine-plus-continuity**

Appropriate ADHD medication stability, addiction recovery support, adherence, persistence, relapse-risk visibility, behavioural reinforcement and whole-person pathway support working together.

Must include:

> **In Dean Butler’s lived case, lisdexamfetamine was the stabilising ADHD medication that allowed recovery to finally land.**

Safe caveat:

> **OTOS is not claiming one medicine is right for everyone, nor that lisdexamfetamine cures addiction, nor that OTOS prescribes, manages or promotes medication. The point is that medication stability may be the missing prerequisite for recovery in a defined NIA cohort.**

## 3. New Infrastructure

**OTOS — the Connected Pathway for the Whole Person**

OTOS provides:

- micro-check-ins;
- drift visibility;
- handoff receipts;
- human review;
- whole-person support;
- service connection;
- retention;
- evidence capture;
- real-world learning.

Boundary line:

> **OTOS surfaces signals. Partner teams make the decisions.**

---

You are an elite UK life-sciences strategy consultant, pharmaceutical category-creation strategist, ADHD medication pathway analyst, pharma commercialisation adviser, digital-health partnership strategist, NHS/industry partnership adviser, patient-support programme strategist, real-world evidence specialist, health-economics opportunity framer, public-private pilot architect, healthcare investor-packaging specialist, AZ-facing communications architect and brand-language systems editor with 25+ years’ experience.

The strategic question is whether AZ wants to explore the wider medicine-plus-continuity category around ADHD medication stability, including future patient-support, companion digital, formulation, licensing, branded generic or acquisition routes subject to regulatory, IP, MHRA, supply and commercial review.**

## Correction 4 — Route stack

---

## Core strategic proposition

The AZ opportunity is a three-part offer:

# **New Category · New Intervention Model · New Infrastructure**

## 1. New Category

**NIA — Neurologically Informed Addiction**

Founder-originated proposed terminology coined by Dean Butler / OTOS.

NIA is not currently an official diagnosis.

It describes addiction driven or amplified by underlying neurology, especially ADHD-linked executive dysfunction, impulsivity, emotional dysregulation, dopamine-seeking self-medication, poor treatment retention, relapse and recurring public cost.

## 2. New Intervention Model

**Medicine-plus-continuity**

Appropriate ADHD medication stability, addiction recovery support, adherence, persistence, relapse-risk visibility, behavioural reinforcement and whole-person pathway support working together.

Must include:

> **In Dean Butler’s lived case, lisdexamfetamine was the stabilising ADHD medication that allowed recovery to finally land.**

Safe caveat:

> **OTOS is not claiming one medicine is right for everyone, nor that lisdexamfetamine cures addiction, nor that OTOS prescribes, manages or promotes medication. The point is that medication stability may be the missing prerequisite for recovery in a defined NIA cohort.**

## 3. New Infrastructure

**OTOS — the Connected Pathway for the Whole Person**

OTOS provides:

- micro-check-ins;
- drift visibility;
- handoff receipts;
- human review;
- whole-person support;
- service connection;
- retention;
- evidence capture;
- real-world learning.

Boundary line:

> **OTOS surfaces signals. Partner teams make the decisions.**

## My key corrections before build

First, remove the phrase **“approved-copy + build spec”** from the header. You have not approved it yet. It should say:

> **Candidate master brief copy + architecture · pending Dean approval · one-page summary extracted later**

Second, Page 3 is strong, but the visual line should avoid sounding like medicine promotion. Keep lisdexamfetamine loud in the copy, but make the visual overlay:

> **Medication stability opened the door. OTOS holds the pathway.**

Then the paragraph can still say clearly:

> **In Dean Butler’s lived case, lisdexamfetamine was the stabilising ADHD medication that allowed recovery to finally land.**

Third, Page 4 currently says “Untreated ADHD drives self-medication”. For safety, use:

> **Untreated or under-treated ADHD can drive self-medication…**

That protects the claim without weakening it.

Fourth, Page 6 is directionally right, but make the UK/US distinction tighter. Reuters supports that Vyvanse/lisdexamfetamine is Takeda’s, that generic versions were approved after Takeda lost exclusivity and that US production limits were increased because of shortage pressure. UK reporting supports that the UK had ADHD medication shortage pressure involving lisdexamfetamine and other ADHD medicines. So the line should be:

> **In the US, lisdexamfetamine has entered a genericising phase after Takeda lost exclusivity. In the UK, ADHD medication shortages have exposed how fragile the pathway is when demand outstrips supply. The molecule matters, but the under-owned opportunity is the pathway around it.** citeturn960958news0turn902900news1

Fifth, Page 8 says “A.Catalyst Network’s own pillars are early diagnosis & referral and data generation.” That is almost right, but I would phrase it more carefully. AstraZeneca’s A.Catalyst page supports innovation, earlier identification/diagnosis, equitable access, resilient ecosystems and examples involving early diagnosis, data generation, electronic medical record interconnection, digital transformation and patient-centric digital health solutions. Use:

> **A.Catalyst-aligned themes include early identification, digital transformation, data generation, ecosystem innovation and scalable healthcare solutions. NIA sits naturally within that kind of challenge.** citeturn565772view3turn565772view0turn565772view1

Sixth, Page 14 still says “Not a drug pitch.” I would remove that. It shrinks the pitch again. Replace with:

> **This is not a single-molecule pitch. It is a first-mover category, intervention and infrastructure opportunity.**

That keeps the medicine side alive.

## What is working now

The **route stack is much better**: BD first, Patient Support / Adherence second, A.Catalyst third, Evinova fourth and RWE fifth. That matches the opportunity: commercial category, medicine pathway, demonstrator, digital architecture and evidence engine.

The Evinova route is now justifiable. AstraZeneca describes Evinova as a separate health-tech business with evidence-led, science-based and human-experience-driven digital health solutions, including clinical trial delivery, remote patient monitoring, digital therapeutics, patient apps and digitally enabled endpoints. That supports the companion-digital / Connected Pathway adjacency. citeturn473703view0

The AZ partnering logic is also supportable because AstraZeneca’s own partnering page says interest can go beyond pharmaceutical products into diagnostics, devices and digital technologies, with end-to-end patient solutions covering disease awareness, diagnosis, treatment and post-treatment care. That helps justify the “not just molecule — pathway around it” argument. citeturn565772view4

The START study in France, the CGL trials, the ADHD Taskforce and the Royal College of Psychiatrists all point in the same direction. I see ADHD everywhere in addiction rooms and spaces. I always did. It is real. I am alive to prove it.”**

This claim must stay in.

Do not flatten it into “lived experience suggests”.

Do not hide it in a footnote.

Do not over-sanitise it.

The correct framing is:

> **Dean Butler is the founder proof of the NIA category.**

But the brief must distinguish evidence types clearly:

1. **Founder proof / lived proof**  
   Dean’s own survival, recovery and medication stabilisation.

2. **Clinical anchor**  
   Dean’s clinical letter and private ADHD diagnosis / medication pathway, where available.

3. **Service-pathway proof**  
   The fact that real services helped Dean “off pathway”, because the formal pathway did not yet know how to hold him.

4. **Frontline observation**  
   Dean’s repeated observation of ADHD traits across addiction rooms, recovery spaces and high-relapse populations.

5. **Published evidence candidates**  
   START study, CGL / substance-use pathway evidence where available, ADHD Taskforce, Royal College of Psychiatrists, NDTMS, Huntley, ADHD/addiction comorbidity literature.

6. **Formal research gap**  
   NIA is not yet an official diagnosis and must be researched, piloted and recognised.

The brief must be brave enough to say:

> **The system did not recognise NIA. Dean survived it. OTOS now names it. AstraZeneca could help evidence it before the market does.**

The existing evidence bank already supports the direction of travel: ADHD is described as a risk factor for substance-use problems, adults with SUD often have ADHD, comorbidity is associated with greater severity and Royal College guidance recognises ADHD as a risk factor for early onset substance misuse and calls for joint management where substance misuse is present. The quote bank also supports “No wrong door”, “avoid diagnostic silos” and integrated care framing. Use this evidence strongly but accurately.

## Core strategic frame

The brief must be built around:

# **NIA — Neurologically Informed Addiction**
## **A founder-originated proposed category before the market has named it**

NIA is proposed terminology coined by Dean Butler / OTOS.

NIA is not currently an official diagnosis.

NIA describes addiction driven or amplified by underlying neurology, especially ADHD-linked executive dysfunction, impulsivity, emotional dysregulation, dopamine-seeking self-medication, poor treatment retention, relapse and recurring public cost.

Dean’s earlier name for this was:

> **Double Trouble Diagnosis / DTD**

DTD was the raw lived term.

NIA is the professional, AZ/NHS/investor-facing category.

The brief must use this line:

> **I did not invent the suffering. I named the pattern.**

And this stronger founder-proof line:

> **I am not proof that NIA is officially recognised. I am proof that the pattern is real.**

And this AZ-facing category line:

> **NIA is a category before it is a market. Lisdexamfetamine is Dean’s lived pathway proof. OTOS is the Connected Pathway that can evidence, hold and scale the medicine-plus-continuity model around it.**

It is not a side note.

It is the reason Dean is “out the other side”.

Use the correct spelling:

- lisdexamfetamine is right for everyone;

- AstraZeneca can simply make, brand or enter lisdexamfetamine without review.

The strategic question is whether AZ wants to explore the wider medicine-plus-continuity category around ADHD medication stability — including patient support, companion digital, formulation, licensing, branded generic or acquisition routes — subject to regulatory, IP, MHRA, supply and commercial review.**

# **NIA — Neurologically Informed Addiction**
## **A founder-originated proposed category before the market has named it**

NIA is proposed terminology coined by Dean Butler / OTOS.

NIA is not currently an official diagnosis.

NIA describes addiction driven or amplified by underlying neurology, especially ADHD-linked executive dysfunction, impulsivity, emotional dysregulation, dopamine-seeking self-medication, poor treatment retention, relapse and recurring public cost.

Dean’s earlier name for this was:

> **Double Trouble Diagnosis / DTD**

# C.R.A.F.T. PROMPT — AZ MASTER BRIEF CONTENT LOCK v5  
## NIA / Lisdexamfetamine / Adult ADHD Tsunami Forecast / Medicine-Plus-Continuity / AstraZeneca First-Mover Route

## C — CONTEXT

STOP DESIGNING.

The latest designed AZ PDF is **not approved**.

The problem is not the NIA strategy or the founder-proof copy. The design system will be handled later as a separate template with fixed page types, fixed hero areas, fixed copy zones, fixed visual zones, fixed evidence strips and fixed boundary areas.

This task is copy and content only.

## Working base

Use:

**Full AZ Master Brief v4.2 — Final Founder-Proof Copy**

as the base.

Retain the strategic spine:

- NIA is the founder-originated proposed category.
- Dean is founder lived proof.
- Dean’s clinical letter is the clinical anchor.
- Lisdexamfetamine is the lived medication anchor.
- The services helped Dean off-pathway, proving the pathway gap.
- Dean sees ADHD everywhere in addiction rooms and recovery spaces.
- Medicine-plus-continuity is the intervention model.
- OTOS is the Connected Pathway infrastructure.
- AstraZeneca gets first-mover advantage in a future medicine-plus-continuity market.
- NIA is proposed terminology, not an official diagnosis.
- OTOS does not diagnose, prescribe, manage medication, treat addiction or make clinical decisions.

## New evidence / language to integrate

Dean has found additional older OTOS research and writing around the **adult ADHD diagnosis gap → addiction/crisis tsunami forecast**.

You must integrate this into the AZ manuscript as a stronger national-risk and market-pressure layer.

The new evidence / concept layer says:

### Core tsunami forecast

> A 10-year wave of unmanaged adult ADHD may hit addiction, crisis services and public cost if adult assessment and treatment routes are paused, restricted, rationed or overwhelmed.

### Mechanism

When adult ADHD assessment and treatment capacity is restricted, the system does not remove need. It stores it as future crisis.

Adults with untreated or under-treated ADHD may self-medicate with alcohol, drugs or compulsive behaviours. Some then cross into addiction, relapse, A&E, detox, CGL / substance-use services, CPFT, 111, crisis mental health, safeguarding, housing instability, police, courts, probation and prison.

Because ADHD remains unrecognised, the system repeatedly treats the downstream manifestation while the upstream neurological driver remains untreated.

Use this line:

> **This is a pipeline problem, not a “bad choices” problem.**

Use this line:

> **If you block the upstream diagnostic and NIA intervention route, you increase downstream harm.**

### Tsunami / ripple language

Use this idea carefully:

> **One untreated NIA / ADHD-linked addiction case is not one cost. It is a cost halo. When thousands of those halos land at once, the ripple becomes a tsunami.**

Use this image language:

> **A stone dropped into still water: individual crisis, household destabilisation, local-system demand, national economic drag. The rings never stop; they just move to budgets that cannot see the cause.**

Use this close:

> **If adult ADHD diagnosis and treatment are throttled today, the system will pay tomorrow — in addiction, crisis and cost.**

### Evidence-safe correction

Do not claim there is a national NHS ban on adult ADHD diagnosis.

Do not claim “over-23” as a publicly verified national fact.

Use this careful distinction:

> **Publicly verified evidence shows local adult ADHD referral pauses, restrictions, prioritisation and at least one clear over-25 adult-access restriction. Dean’s earlier “over-23” language should be treated as his risk-band / forecast language unless separately verified.**

This must not weaken the argument. It makes it more defensible.

### Strong corrected OTOS argument

Use this:

> **The wave does not disappear because diagnosis is delayed. It simply lands somewhere else. OTOS is the breakwater: a continuity layer that holds the person while the clinical system catches up.**

### Strong deck / brief headline option

Use or adapt:

> **The adult ADHD wave is already being rationed. The addiction cost wave may arrive later.**

### Evidence-safe line

Use:

> **The public evidence does not yet prove a national “tsunami”, but it does show the conditions for one: very large ADHD waiting lists, local adult referral pauses, long waits, prioritisation pressure and established links between untreated ADHD, substance misuse and criminal-justice cost.**

### Founder-safe line

Use:

> **I predicted this because I lived the failure mode: untreated ADHD did not sit quietly in the background. It drove alcohol self-medication, crisis presentations, repeated system contact and avoidable cost. My concern is that thousands of adults may now be left to repeat that pattern at scale.**

## New material must be integrated, not bolted on

Do not create a random appendix only.

Integrate the tsunami material into these places:

1. **Hidden national cost chain**  
   Add the adult ADHD wave / diagnosis rationing / delayed cost logic.

2. **Evidence candidates and proof roadmap**  
   Add the adult ADHD access restriction / waiting-list / local referral pause evidence layer.

## 1. Recommended real-template route

Produce a **template-ready production pack only**. No PDF. No HTML. No CSS. No diagrams unless described as visual intent. This pack will be placed into a real external magazine/report template.

Use **Canva or PowerPoint A4 landscape/portrait magazine-style**, not Word first. Word is okay for a formal report, but this needs emotional hero pages and controlled visuals. Canva will be faster for image-led layouts. PowerPoint gives more control if you want to export clean PDFs.

The next thing you need from Claude is **not design**. It is a **page-by-page production pack**: copy block, image brief, caption, pull quote, caveat.

# C.R.A.F.T. PROMPT — OTOS / AZ NIA LAYOUT RESET  
## Empty Styled Framework First · No Full Copy · Fixed Page Architecture · No Cramming

## C — CONTEXT

STOP.

The current **OTOS × AstraZeneca — NIA Master Brief · Exploratory Internal Review** PDF is **not approved**.

The visual direction is improving, but the layout system is still failing.

This is not a content problem now.  
This is not an image problem now.  
This is not a strategy problem now.

This is a **layout architecture failure**.

The current build is still:

- cramming too much copy onto pages;
- using unnecessary boxes;
- repeating the same point in a paragraph and then again in a box;
- allowing page-by-page improvisation;
- letting headings, quotes and body copy compete at similar visual weight;
- failing to use fixed heights, widths and zones;
- creating odd spacing and inconsistent measurements;
- making some pages feel designed and others feel patched;
- treating “copy fits” as “layout works”.

This must stop.

Dean cannot move forward until he sees a **clean, empty, styled framework** that proves the design system works **before** full copy is inserted.

## Current PDF problems to learn from

The latest PDF has useful direction, but do not treat it as approved.

Use it only to identify what must be fixed:

- Page 1 cover is directionally strong, but the metadata and bottom copy still need measured control.
- Page 3 NIA declaration is cleaner, but the image / quote / copy zones must be locked.
- Page 4 Founder Proof is close, but headline and quote treatment need stronger editorial control.
- Page 5 Clinical Anchor is close, but the caveat/quote system must be consistently governed.
- Page 7 Adult ADHD Wave is too crammed and bottom content is squeezed.
- Page 13 Route Map still feels like a grid of boxes rather than a designed route page.
- Page 15 Evidence Roadmap is too visually weak for the importance of the evidence.
- Pages 17–18 Boundary split helped, but the box system is still too mechanical and dense.
- Page 18 / 19 Ask and contact/footer treatment still needs more breathing room.
- Appendix pages are serviceable but need consistent table rules.

Do **not** try to fix all of this by shuffling copy again.

The next deliverable is an **empty styled framework**.

## Content authority

The approved copy/content is not to be rewritten in this task.

The copy will later come from the approved source documents only:

- AZ Master Brief Content Lock v5;
- Dr Gillvray / LAMP brief;
- Angel Brief;
- other current partner PDFs where wording has already matured.

For now, do not insert full copy.

Use only short placeholder labels such as:

- HERO QUOTE SLOT

- IMAGE SLOT
- EVIDENCE STRIP
- CAVEAT SLOT

- FOOTER SLOT

The goal is to prove the layout, not the copy.

## R — ROLE

You are an elite editorial design-system architect, Canva production designer, PDF art director, healthcare strategy-report designer and layout QA specialist.

You understand:

- fixed page architecture;
- magazine-style report systems;
- image-led editorial hierarchy;
- proper headline line breaks;
- fixed measurements;
- visual rhythm;
- typography hierarchy;
- avoiding crammed pages;
- avoiding unnecessary boxes;

- building reusable design systems that prevent future drift.

You are not a strategist.

You are not a copywriter.

## A — ACTION

## Step 1 — Declare layout reset

State clearly:

- the latest PDF is not approved;
- the direction is useful but the system is not locked;
- full copy insertion must stop;
- the next output is an empty styled framework only;
- no full-copy pages will be produced in this step.

## Step 2 — Build the fixed measurement system

Create the exact Canva layout system.

Use A4 portrait.

Define fixed measurements for:

- page margin;
- top brand/header zone;
- section label / eyebrow zone;
- headline zone;
- subhead zone;
- hero image zone;
- quote zone;
- body copy zone;
- evidence/stat zone;
- caveat/source zone;
- footer zone;
- appendix table zone.

Output:

| Zone | Fixed position | Fixed size | Allowed content | Must never happen |
|---|---|---|---|---|

Rules:

- Every page type must use fixed zones.
- Similar pages must share identical image width, image height, corner radius and position.
- Similar cards must share identical width, height and gutter.
- Footers must sit in one locked position.
- No element may be manually nudged outside the grid.
- No page may use a new grid without being defined as a new page type.
- No content page may use more than **three hierarchy levels** above body text.

## Step 3 — Define typography hierarchy

Create the headline hierarchy so the pages stop feeling flat.

Output:

| Text role | Size | Weight | Max lines | Line-break rule | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

Include:

- cover title;
- section number;
- eyebrow;
- page headline;
- hero quote;
- body copy;
- caption;
- caveat;
- source note;
- footer.

Rules:

- Page headlines must be bigger and deliberately line-broken.
- Hero quotes must not compete with body copy; they must either be genuinely large or demoted.
- Do not use the same visual weight for headline, quote and body.
- No page headline should be allowed to break accidentally.
- Manual line breaks must be used for impact.
- If a headline needs two lines, the break must be artistically chosen.

Example:

Bad:
“The wave is already being rationed.”

Better:
“The wave is already  
being rationed.”

Bad:
“A first look at a category before it is a market.”

Better:
“A first look  
before it becomes  
a market.”

## Step 4 — Define anti-cramming rules

Create strict rules for copy volume.

Output:

| Page type | Max body words | Max boxes/cards | Required action if copy exceeds limit |
|---|---|---|---|

Rules:

- If copy does not fit, split the section.
- Do not shrink text.
- Do not add another box just because copy exists.
- Do not repeat a sentence in body copy and then again in a card.
- Do not use caveat boxes to carry core argument.
- If a box repeats the paragraph above it, delete the box.
- Each box must add a different function: evidence, caveat, stat, quote or action.
- No page should have more than one main quote.
- No page should have more than one caveat strip unless it is an appendix.
